steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks what both authors would agree on. Text 1 is confident that Rasht's designation brought awareness and a tourism boost. Text 2 acknowledges the designation's potential but stresses that cities need active marketing to realize full benefits. The correct answer must be a claim both authors would accept — it cannot contradict either text.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — The benefits of Rasht having been named a City of Gastronomy extend well beyond increased tourism.: Neither text mentions benefits beyond tourism and awareness of local food culture. Text 2 in particular focuses only on food tourism. This goes beyond what either author claims. ✗
  • Choice B — Increased tourism isn't guaranteed after a city has been named a City of Gastronomy.: Text 1 explicitly states the designation provided a boost to Rasht's tourism industry — treating increased tourism as something that already happened, not as uncertain. This contradicts Text 1. ✗
  • Choice C — A city's food scene can benefit from the city being named a City of Gastronomy.: Text 1 confirms this happened in Rasht (awareness, tourism boost). Text 2 agrees this is possible but requires marketing effort. The word "can" allows for both perspectives: actual benefit (Text 1) and potential benefit contingent on strategy (Text 2). ✓
  • Choice D — A significant number of visitors to Rasht may not know that it was named a City of Gastronomy.: Text 1 directly states the honor is well known among tourists who visit, contradicting the idea that visitors may not know about it. ✗
That matches Choice C — A city's food scene can benefit from the city being named a City of Gastronomy..
